unit.name: Evaluating parses and moving to neural and graph-based parsers
unit.description: Defines UAS/LAS, motivates neural transition-based parsing with embeddings and a multilayer classifier, and ends with graph-based parsing as a more accurate alternative.
unit.summary: The final segment first explains parser evaluation. A gold dependency tree is compared against a predicted tree by checking whether each word gets the correct head; unlabeled dependency accuracy measures head correctness, while labeled accuracy additionally checks relation labels such as subject or object [ts=3870s] [ts=3966s]. The lecture then motivates neural parsing by noting that symbolic features are sparse, incomplete, and expensive to compute, so neural models can replace brittle local conjunctions with dense learned representations [ts=3987s] [ts=4027s]. The neural transition-based parser embeds words, parts of speech, and dependency labels, extracts the key stack and buffer items, concatenates their vectors, passes them through hidden layers with ReLU, and uses a softmax to predict shift, left-arc, or right-arc [ts=4164s] [ts=4337s]. This yields a nonlinear classifier that was both accurate and fast enough to scale, leading to later beam-search improvements like Parsey McParseface [ts=4341s] [ts=4470s]. The lecture closes by sketching graph-based parsing, where each possible dependency arc is scored and a tree algorithm such as minimum spanning tree selects a well-formed parse; these models were slower earlier on but can be more accurate, and the speaker notes neural graph-based parsing as the more accurate method in Stanza [ts=4520s] [ts=4716s].

key_points.text: Unlabeled dependency accuracy checks whether each word's head is correct, while labeled accuracy also checks the dependency label.
Symbolic parsing features were sparse, incomplete, and expensive enough that feature computation dominated runtime.
The neural parser embeds words, POS tags, and dependency labels from the stack and buffer, concatenates them, and feeds them to a neural network.
A softmax over parser actions predicts shift, left-arc, and right-arc, giving a nonlinear transition classifier.
Graph-based parsing scores candidate arcs for each word and then selects a tree, for example with a minimum spanning tree algorithm.

kp_catalog.name: UAS and LAS evaluation
kp_catalog.description: Standard parsing metrics that compare predicted dependency trees to gold trees by head correctness alone or by head-plus-label correctness.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because uAS and LAS evaluation clarifies standard parsing metrics that compare predicted dependency trees to gold trees by head correctness alone or by head-plus-label correctness, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Neural transition-based parser
kp_catalog.description: A transition parser that replaces sparse symbolic features with embeddings and a nonlinear neural classifier over parser actions.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because neural transition-based parser synthesizes a transition parser that replaces sparse symbolic features with embeddings and a nonlinear neural classifier over parser actions into an end-to-end method or integrated recipe.

kp_catalog.name: Local parser state and features
kp_catalog.description: The representation of a parser decision state using the stack, buffer, nearby words, parts of speech, dependency labels, and other local structural cues.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because local parser state and features clarifies the representation of a parser decision state using the stack, buffer, nearby words, parts of speech, dependency labels, and other local structural cues, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Graph-based dependency parsing
kp_catalog.description: A parsing approach that scores candidate arcs globally and then selects a well-formed tree, often with a tree optimization algorithm.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because graph-based dependency parsing extends the core thread to a parsing approach that scores candidate arcs globally and then selects a well-formed tree, often with a tree optimization algorithm without being required for the main pipeline.
